speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three. The survivor is Samuel Friedman. You were telling us about the teacher's college in the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The teacher's college was in Krzemieniec. We enrolled. We were accepted. And in-- probably in September, '40, we started studying. We got a scholarship of 180 ruble, which was good enough. I paid for my room, sharing with my friend, 30 ruble per month. And the rest was good for food and some other things which we needed. We had-- we used the cafeteria, the university's cafeteria because very inexpensive and excellent food, excellent Rus-- Russian food. And so the lessons were four or five hours in the morning. And then we had to do some homework. And we were in the class a bunch of students from different universities, as I mentioned. And we were much, much better equipped than the Russian ones who came from Kiev or from other places. We knew much more and much better than the Russians. So we were the elite. Still, in a Russian subject like the Communist subjects, which we had to study as well-- Marxism and Leninism, a bit of, the history of the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means the Communist Party. But these were subjects which we were not very much interested in. But still, we had to do it. The main thing was physics, mathematics, and chemistry and methods of teaching physics, methods of teaching mathematics, and methods, and so on. And the Russian teachers, some of them are really geniuses. But some of the are completely uneducated. We had such a thing like at this time, Russia was in war with Finland in '40s. And the Finns were very, very brave. And they defended the Mannerheim Line.

speaker: interviewee
text: But eventually, the Russians overcome them because-- with the mass of millions of soldiers and how many victims there were. And they took over the province of Karelia. So the history teacher, who wasn't very well-educated probably, asked us once-- well, we were talking about Korea. And she asked and the Korean war and the Korean War with the Japanese and the Korean war, and to whom belongs Korea today? So everybody says Korea belongs to Japan. No, you don't know. So she mentioned again, to whom belongs Korea today? Everybody said to Japan. And now everybody was alert. What's going on here? Now is something going. So and she insisted that Korea belongs to Russia. And we insisted than Korea-- of course, she thought the Karelia. the Karelian Republic of Finland, which they took over, belonged-- belonged-- was occupied by Russia and not Korea.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a big thing. And we spoke to the headmaster about this. And, and I think  she left later on, this woman. This was a woman-- well, just an incident from the school. And we graduated in June, '41, just in time to see the Germans invading in Barbarossa, Operation Barbarossa. And then it was all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much of a surprise was that invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, it was a complete surprise. I read the Russian papers. I was listening to the Russian radio, which was one voice from all over the Soviet Union. And on Sunday morning, we had some military exercise from school, from the teacher's college. And then we got the, the certificates and things like this. But I came back home at 11:00, 12:00, lunch time. And we heard tanks rolling through the main street. But you could hear the tanks. It was a noise, enormous noise. And we went to see what is happening. And the tanks were rolling and rolling, the T-34s, the huge tanks. And then we didn't know what's happening. We had not the slightest idea. And then Molotov was speaking over the radio-- not Stalin, Molotov. And Molotov was announcing that Germany has invaded the Soviet Union. And of course, we will fight and so on, so on, so on, so on. On the third of July, we saw the Germans coming in town. Well, it wasn't good. They didn't start anything. But they took-- after a few days-- maybe a week or two-- they start taking the Jews to work. And one day, I went with the group of Jews to work for the Germans or the Ukrainians. Because the Ukrainians are mainly the, the ones who were in charge. And it was beating and shoving. And, and, and, it was-- nobody was killed. But still, it was the atmosphere, well, you are not a human being anymore. You are absolutely not human being. They can do with you whatever they, they want. And next time when we were called by the Jewish [INAUDIBLE], is anybody the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], the Jewish authority. There was some kind of Jewish authority in town-- to come to work, I said to my friend, a friend of mine, I'm not going.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, why don't you go? When I listen, I can see this terrible. I am trying to escape. And he said to me, listen, what will happen to all the Jews will happen to us. We will go all together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was-- I wasn't very, very excited about this and already convinced that I can do. But I still did-- said, I can't. I don't. I don't. And then I started looking for a place how to escape. And I found some Ukrainians who-- first thing, I wasn't wearing the Jewish, whatever they were wearing then. And secondly, I went for a, for a permit to go to Warsaw. And I couldn't-- my nerve wasn't good enough to stay and wait for the permit in the, in the, in the Gestapo. It was a kind to Gestapo organization who, who give permits to travel. And I went out. And then I went to some Ukrainians who advised me that there is-- no, this was probably Polish men-- [INAUDIBLE] remembers this-- advised me that in this and this town or village, I can establish myself as a teacher. They need teachers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I start walking. And I walked and I walked and I walked until I arrived to a village. I met some Polish people. They had children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was very close to winter already. And these people established-- these people wanted me to stay with them as a teacher. So I taught the children, whatever, simple things-- reading and arithmetics and things like this. And then I tried to establish myself with the Ukrainians as a teacher in the village. But I couldn't. And then came the, the nobility. It was two, two ladies, two old ladies. One was probably in the 80s. One was in the 60s, something like-- mother and sister, mother and daughter. They were living in her, in their very large house in a village. They were the owners of the village, anyway. And they wanted me to stay with them. And so I came to stay with them for the winter. And I left this teaching. It was to communicate and to, to-- no transport, only walking in the snow, in deep snow. It was impossible. So I stayed with the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I can't remember the name of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in hiding by this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know if you can call it hiding. I didn't say anybody that I am Jewish. On the contrary, I said to everybody that I am Polish. And I am a teacher, and I-- so and so. And they accepted me. The whole thing here was that I looked Polish. I didn't look Jewish. Oh, you asked how come that so many Jews couldn't escape, couldn't hide, couldn't go. They couldn't, because they were recognized in the street in the meantime-- in the anytime. And I was-- I looked like Polish. You can see on the picture, this with my hat. And, and nobody was suspecting me. And my Polish was perfect. My Polish was very, very good, Polish, Polish, so nobody-- still, I was very much cautious not to do something, or not say something which will show that I am Jewish. So once, having a dinner with the family-- and dinner was served very elegantly in the dining room and some servants were around-- and she asked me if I liked onion. Ah, you'll have some onion.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, oh, no, no. I don't like onions. You know, onions is a Jewish-- you know about this or no? Yeah. Onions is-- Jews usually use onions. So I said, no, no, no, no. I, I don't like onions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, it's probably you're forefathers used too mu-- too much. That's why you don't like it. Now, this was the case. Still, it was very innocent. It was probably some kind of talk. And for me, it was a, a-- quite a shock. Whatever I say is not good. I don't onions, it's not good. I like onions-- anyway, we went together to the church in Shumsk in Ukraine. We used to go once a week, once two weeks. And then in Christmas time, I remember, we went to Shumsk. And I stayed with them. I did what they did. And nothing was bothering me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, I got-- I spoke to a Ukrainian, one Ukrainian, who told me he came back from Germany. He got a certificate to go to see his family. And I asked him, what do you do in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, I was working on a farm. And it's very quiet, very good, plenty of food. And people are working from Ukraine, from Poland, from others are working there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, you know what, are you going back? No, I am not going back. So give me your certificate. Maybe I will do something with this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, he wanted some kerosene. Well, I had some kerosene in my room. Because we were using kerosene. So I gave him some kerosene. I was dragging the kerosene over the snow and frost and things. Anyway, I took this certificate, which later on was absolutely useless. And I decided to go to Germany. But I wanted to be in Warsaw before, because I knew my parents were in ghetto Warsaw. During my stay in Russia, during the couple of years I stayed in Russia, I got letters from my parents. One of the photographs when I show my parents, my sister, and my girlfriend came from Warsaw ghetto. And the mail was normal. I could write letters. They wrote letters. And I have three letters here written by my mother and father and my girlfriend. I have got the letters here from them. I got them in the Ukraine from Warsaw ghetto. Because the ghetto wasn't so closed. You, you didn't know exactly what's going on. So I decided to go. And I came to a town of Dubno, to the railway station. Because the railways were not going everywhere. So from the villages, I came to the Dubno. And from here, I wanted to take train to go to Warsaw. And I came to the station. It was frost, enormous frost. Station was deserted. Only military transports were going. And no one was even trying to go by train anywhere. But I found a Polish man who was running around. And, and he met me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, where are you going? So I told him, I want to go to Warsaw. Oh, I am from Lwow. You know Lwow? I want to go to  my family in Lwow. But I can't talk to them. They don't want-- you speak German? Yes, I speak German. So I went to ask the German gendarme, Can we take a train to Lwow? Military transports, what are you talking? Get away from here. I don't want to see you, and so on. So I was running around. And then I saw in one of the wagons, one of the carriages, soldiers with different uniform, completely different uniform. So I went to, to talk to the soldiers. They were Italian soldiers. The Italian soldiers were transporting convoy from Romania to the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were guards. This was a guard-- food or beverages or whatever they were transporting-- wine, I think. And this was on-- this was the way they were going back. In Dubno, I found them going back to-- for a new transport to Romania.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I tried to, to speak to them. But I don't speak Italian. But I found-- I asked if somebody speaks French. So we found a sergeant who spoke French. And I could communicate with him easily. And I explained to him that we are going to families, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. So he says, well, get on the, the train. And we, we will go through Lwow, because we are going through Lwow to Romania. And I told this Polish man. And he was happy, because he couldn't speak German. He couldn't speak French. He was happy to go with me. So we got on the train. And there were some 15 or 20 Italians. And one German was sitting in the middle with a fire, you know, fire lit in the middle of the, of the, of the carriage, because it was cold. And the German was absolutely out of, of, of his mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: He just spoke about 52 degrees in [INAUDIBLE]. And now he's going to Danzig to see his family. He wasn't interested in me, where I am going, what I am doing, not at all. Anyway, in this train, I wanted to see what's happening. So I took from one of the, oh, the Italians, his helmet. So I put-- put my head through the window somewhere and look what is happening. And then the train stopped moving. And once the train started moving, I saluted German gendarme who was standing. And he saluted me back. And so I-- out from the Ukraine. And then we came to Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Ukraine, before we leave the Ukraine, in those two years you lived there, what do you recall about the relationship between the civilians, the locals, and the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the Soviet regime, I didn't know anything about this. I-- nobody was talking about this. Nobody was mentioning this. You mention, you get five years. So it was absolutely nothing, no relationship. We had relationship with our friends from school, Jewish and not Jewish. And we are going out-- I was going  out with a girl, [? Maya ?] [? Podgaet. ?] And she was dancing. And she was a ballet dancer. And she was everything. And then she got married somewhere. But still, we went together. We were spending time on some occasion, whatever. So the, the relationship between us and others was normal, the same citizens of the same country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you recall about the Germans coming in, what they are doing and what they were doing to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't see nothing, because I was in the villages. The first impression was it's not good. They were beating people. They're throwing and pushing. And, and then they brought the Ukrainian guards, which were even worse. It was just take your bag and run away if you can. So I did it. I ran away. I just didn't want to stay with them, which was the right thing to do. But still, not everybody was equipped to do it. Nobody would say that I am Jewish when I came there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came Lwow. And in Lwow, this chap, this Pole, he was so grateful that I, I saved his life. He invited me to his place. And I stayed with him for a while. and then I decided it was-- in Lwow, it was outside-- this was the, they called it, General, General Government. This part of Poland was called General Government. It was different. It was free. Everybody was running. Everybody was buying, and selling, and working. And transport was operating. It was-- in Ukraine was dead. But here, it was rather-- so I decided to catch the train and go to Krakow. And from Krakow, I was thinking to go to Warsaw. Well, I got the train. And I had this piece of paper from this Ukrainian, which I thought it would save me in case of-- it didn't do nothing. I never showed it. I don't have it here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came to Krakow. And in Krakow, I had to deliver some letters from the, from the two ladies to the husband of the older lady who was a very well-known solicitor in Krakow. Still, I don't remember it. I don't remember his name. Maybe it was Kowalski or something like this. But I can't recall the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I gave him the letter. But he didn't invite me. But still, I stayed with the friends of the Polish man from Lwow. And I stayed with them for a while. And then I saw the ghetto in Krakow. I came close to the ghetto, just wanted investigate what's going on. And my impression was that the people were coming and going and going and coming. They had-- the Jews had the, the blue and white armbands if I recall correctly. But still, they had some sign they are Jews. But they could go out. And it was movement-- free movement.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I understood, well, it's, it's not so bad. Maybe I can get into the Warsaw ghetto and see my parents and communicate and do something. So I decided to go to Warsaw after a few days. On the 13th of January, I left the Ukraine. Five days later, six days later, I was in Warsaw, Warsaw. What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: '42, January '42. When the train approached Warsaw, this was getting dark. You know, in Poland in winter, at four o'clock, it's dark. The police hour or the curfew was, I think, eight o'clock. Till eight o'clock, you have to find your way. Otherwise, you are dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we approach Warsaw and I saw the suburbs, I got very, very nervous. Where I am going to stay or where to go? And I knew that the curfew is eight o'clock or something like this. So I asked the man who was sitting next to me-- I told him a story about I am just coming from the Ukraine. I was caught, whatever the story was. I can't remember. Anyway, tell me if you have somebody where I can stay a few days until I find my work, my family, my everything. And he gave me an address.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave me an address, a couple, older couple, who's son was working in the embassy, the Polish embassy, in London. What he did, I don't know, this vice-consul or something. It wasn't the ambassador. And he said they were very helpful people. The names, I can't remember anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And tell them that I sent you. And my name is Kowalski. Now, Kowalski is John Smith. And can you imagine who is John Smith? So anyway, I found the address. I went. It was dark. And I knocked on the door. They didn't want to open. And I spoke through the-- Mr. Kowalski sent me. What Kowalski? What Kowalski? We don't let-- but still, he opened the door. Once he opened the door, I put my foot as [INAUDIBLE]. Because it was desperate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to get into the, too the house, into the flat, it was death. They will catch you in a minute. And the, the winter was cold, minus 15, minus-- 20. Not terrible, but minus 20 at night is unpleasant. Once he opened this, I started talking nicely Polish. And then, oh, no I can't-- but maybe you can sleep in the kitchen on the table, on the table in the kitchen. So I came in. Once I came in, I took the overcoat, the heavy overcoat, and the hat and everything out. And I was rather more human, more, more civilized. So they talked to me and saying ah, maybe you want something to eat. Ah, well, we'll eat something. I had some money. What money I had, it was Polish, Polish silver money, which I didn't throw away. Because it was worthless in Russia. Russia was rubles only.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here, it was going crazy. So I had some few-- few just to survive [? or so. ?] So they invited me. And I stayed with them. And they asked me questions. What are you going to do? What are you going to do?

words.text: What are you going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told them, I'm looking for something. I use this and this. And then in the morning, from the depths of the corridor, from the passageway, came a man, older man. So he's probably in the 40s. And he started asking me friendly questions. He told me that he is an engineer. And he was kept in a, in a factory, aviation fac-- aviation factory or airplane factory or, or engine factory, whatever it was, in Austria somewhere. And he run away. He didn't want to work there. And he asked me what I am doing, what I-- I was studying chemistry or engineering in France. But on the one [? ear, ?] I don't know nothing. Ah, but you have the idea of working in the laboratory. Oh, yes. Well, I know the pipettes and the burettes and then-- and the different electrical [? polymeter ?] and so on. So oh, I have here an idea. I have here a friend of my friends whose son was prisoner of war, Polish prisoner of war, during the '39 campaign. And he is still there. It's '42. He's still there in Germany. But he is a chemist, graduated chemist. He was an officer in the army. And he is working in a laboratory where is a routine control of the production. There's no  research, no nothing. Because you must have higher education for this. But still, you can operate in a laboratory. This is good. But he wants-- they want, they want relieve him and let him go home if he sends a replacement. If you want to go, we will give you some few hundred marks. I go up out and change and, and papers, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was an opportunity which was not to be missed. And I agreed. And we went to see the parents of this chemist. And they were terribly happy. And I was invited to dinner. And then I was told what I will-- they will give me few hundred marks. And they will give me food and things. And, and I will get his room in Germany. When I replace him, I will get his room. Because he was living somewhere privately. He wasn't a prisoner of war. I must maybe say something about the prisoners of war, Polish prisoners of war. They got-- can I continue? We'll just stop to change the tape for a second.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
